front: What does multiprogramming do?
back: Increases CPU utilization, as well as keeping users satisfied by organizing programs so that the CPU always has one to execute.

front: How does the distinction between kernel mode and user mode function as a rudimentary form of protection (security)?
back: It provides a form of security, in that certain instructions can not be executed unless in kernel mode. Similarly, hardware devices can only be accessed when the program is in kernel mode.

front: Some CPUs provide for more than two modes of operation. What are two possible uses of these multiple modes?

front: Give two reasons why caches are useful. What problems do they solve? What problems do they cause? If a cache can be made as large as the device for which it is caching (for instance, a cache as large as a disk), why not make it that large and eliminate the device?
back: Caches are useful when two or more components need to exchange data, and the components perform transfers at differing speeds.

front: How do clustered systems differ from multiprocessor systems? What is required for two machines belonging to a cluster to cooperate to provide a highly available service?
back: Clustered systems use messages to communicate, while processors in a multiprocessor system could communicate using shared memory

front: Do kernel modules run in user space?
back: No, they run in kernel space. Normal applications run in user space.

front: What is the difference between insmod and modprobe?

front: Do usb mice and sound cards use character devices or block devices?
What does the major number in /dev signify?
back: A unique way oif identifying a driver which can be associated with a particular device device.

front: What does the minor number in /dev signify?
back: The minor number corresponds to the number of instances of the device. A minor number starts at 0 and goes to the number of instaces minus one. Each instance will have its own minor number.

front: What is a race condition?
back: Race conditions are a result of uncontrolled access to shared data.

front: What is locking or mutual exclusion?
back: A technique for access management that makes sure only one thread of execution can manipulate a shared resource at any time.

front: What is a spinlock?
back: A lock that forces a process that is trying to access the lock to wait in a loop while repeatedly checking if the lock is available, until the lock is available.

front: What is a hybrid kernel?

front: What are the three main methods of passing parameters in an operating system?
back: 1) Pass the parameters in registers (this may prove insufficient when there are more parameters than registers). (2) Store the parameters in a block, or table, in memory, and pass the address of block as a parameter in a register. This approach is used by Linux and Solaris. (3) Push the parameters onto a stack; to be popped off by the OS.

front: What does the long-term scheduler (or job scheduler) do?

front: What does the short-term scheduler (or CPU scheduler) do?

front: Which is invoked more often, short-term scheduler or long-term, and what does this mean?

front: What is an orphan process?
back: An orphan process is a computer process whose parent process has finished or terminated, though it remains running itself.

front: What is a zombie process?
back: When no parent is waiting for the child to close, the process is a zombie.

back: Named pipes (aka FIFO) is an extension to ordinary pipes. A named pipe can last longer  than the lifetime of the process, and appears as a file and generally processes attach to it for interprocess communication.

front: What is parallelism?
back: When a system can perform more than one task simultaneously

front: What are the different types of parallelism?
What is the difference between data parallelism and task parallelism?
back: Data parallelism distributes subsets of the same data across multiple cores and does the same operation on each, while task parallelism distributes threads across cores, with each thread performing a unique operation.

front: What is the objective of Amdahl’s Law?
back: Identifying performance gains from adding additional cores to an application

front: What are thread pools?
back: Pools where you create a number of threads where they await work

front: What are signals used for in UNIX systems?

front: What does the dispatcher module do?
back: Give control of the CPU to the process selected by the short-term scheduler.

front: What is dispatch latency?
back: The time it takes for the dispatcher to stop one process and start another running.

front: What is turnaround time? (CPU Scheduling)
back: Amount of time to execute a particular process

front: What is waiting time? (CPU Scheduling)
back: The amount of time a process has been waiting in the ready queue

front: Wait is response time? (CPU Scheduling)
back: The amount of time it takes from when a requ4est was submitted until the first response is produced, not output

front: Which is optimal, first come first serve or shortest-job-first scheduling?
How does Round Robin work in CPU scheduling?
back: Each process gets a small unit of CPU time, and after this time has elapsed the process is preemted and added to the end of the ready queue.

front: How does priority scheduling work?
back: A priority number is associated with each process, and the PCU is allocated the process with the highest priority (the smaller the integer, the higher the priority)

front: What is a problem with priority scheduling, and what is a solution to this problem?
back: Starvation, low priority processes may never execute. The solution is to implement aging, where the priority increases as time progresses.

front: How do soft real-time systems work?
back: Critical real-time tasks have the highest priority, but no guarantee as to when tasks will be scheduled.

front: How do hard real-time systems work?
back: Task must be serviced by its deadline.

front: What is event latency?
back: The amount of time that eleapses from when an event occurs to when it is serviced.

front: What is interrupt latency?
back: Time from arrival of interrupt to start of routine that services interrupt

front: How does earlist deadline first scheduling (EDF) work?
back: Priorities are assigned according to deadlines.  The deadline must be announced by the process with it becomes runnable.

front: What does NUMA stand for?
What is a critical section?
back: A section of the code that is critical, where only one process may be in the section at a time.

front: What is the entry section?
back: The section before the critical section, where each process must ask permission to enter critical section

front: What is the exit section and remainder section?
back: The exit section is the section immediately following the critical section. The remainder section is the section following the exit section.

front: What is a preemptive kernel?
back: Preemptive Kernel, as name suggests, is a type of kernel that always executes the highest priority task that are ready to run. It cannot use-non-reentrant functions unless and until functions are mutual exclusive.

front: What is a non-preemptive kernel?
back: Non-Preemptive Kernel, as name suggests, is a type of kernel that is free from race conditions on kernel data structures as only one process is active in kernel at a time. It is considered as a serious drawback for real time applications as it does not allow preemption of process running in kernel mode.

front: What is memory model?
back: The memory guarantees a computer architecture makes to application programs

front: What does it mean when a memory model is strongly ordered?
back: A memory modification of one processor is immediately visible to all other processors

front: What does it mean when a memory model is weakly ordered?
back: A memory modification of one processor may not be immediately visible to all other processors

front: What is a memory barrier?
back: An instruction that forces any change in memory to be made visible to all other processors

front: What is special about atomic variables?
back: They provide atomic (uninterruptible) updates on basic data types such as integers and booleans.

What is a counting semaphore?
back: An integer value that can range over an unrestricted domain

front: What is a binary semaphore?
back: An integer value that can range between only 0 and 1

front: A binary semaphore is the same as what other locking mechanism?
What is a deadlock?
back: A deadlock is when two or more processes are waiting indefinitely for an event that can be caused by only one of the waiting processes.
